event: Neolithic settlers leave the earliest archaeological traces on the Macao peninsula and Coloane, evidenced by stone tools and pottery found at sites such as Hac Sa.
The Qin dynasty incorporates the region around the Pearl River Delta, including Macao, into the Chinese empire under Panyu county.
Under the Song dynasty, the Macao area is administratively placed under Xiangshan county in Guangdong province.
Refugees and remnants of the fleeing Southern Song court pass through the Pearl River Delta region near Macao during the Mongol conquest.
The A-Ma Temple, dedicated to the sea goddess Mazu, is established, later giving Macao its Portuguese-derived name.
Portuguese explorer Jorge Álvares becomes the first European to reach the South China coast near the Pearl River estuary.
Portuguese traders obtain rights to anchor ships in Macao's harbors in exchange for an annual payment to Ming authorities.
Portuguese merchants gain permission to dry their water-damaged goods on the Macao peninsula, beginning a permanent presence.
The Ming dynasty grants the Portuguese the right to establish a permanent trading settlement at Macao, the first European base in China.
Pope Gregory XIII establishes the Roman Catholic Diocese of Macao, making the city a major center for Christian missionary activity in East Asia.
The Senado (Leal Senado), Macao's first municipal council, is formed to govern the Portuguese community's local affairs.
The Jesuits found the College of St. Paul (Colégio de São Paulo), a pioneering university-level institution in East Asia.
Construction begins on the Church of Mater Dei, whose facade survives today as the Ruins of St. Paul's, an iconic Macao landmark.
Macao's residents repel a Dutch invasion fleet at the Battle of Macau, securing Portuguese control of the port.
Portugal appoints the first Governor of Macao, formalizing royal administration over the colony.
Construction is completed on the Guia Fortress and its fortifications strengthening the city's defenses.
The Qing dynasty opens Macao as a customs port, integrating it into China's regulated maritime trade system.
The Jesuits are expelled from Macao following the suppression of the order across the Portuguese empire.
Macao's printing presses publish A Abelha da China, the first Western-style newspaper in China.
Commissioner Lin Zexu's anti-opium campaign and the outbreak of the First Opium War heighten tensions affecting Macao's trade.
The British establishment of Hong Kong after the Opium War begins to eclipse Macao's role as the dominant regional trading port.
Macao is detached from Portuguese Goa and made a separate overseas province together with Timor and Solor.
Portugal unilaterally declares Macao a free port and stops paying ground rent to China, asserting full colonial sovereignty.
Governor Ferreira do Amaral imposes direct Portuguese administration, expelling Chinese customs officials and taxing local residents.
Governor Ferreira do Amaral is assassinated by Chinese villagers, and Portugal subsequently consolidates control over the surrounding islands.
Portugal extends its control over Taipa island, expanding the territory of Macao.
Portugal extends its control over Coloane island, further enlarging the colony's territory.
The Sino-Portuguese Treaty of Peking formally recognizes Portugal's perpetual occupation and government of Macao.
Portuguese forces clear pirates from Coloane in the Battle of Coloane, the same year Portugal becomes a republic.
Macao becomes a refuge for Chinese fleeing the Second Sino-Japanese War, swelling its population.
Neutral Portuguese Macao remains unoccupied during World War II but suffers severe shortages while sheltering tens of thousands of refugees.
Portugal reclassifies Macao from a colony to an overseas province under the Estado Novo regime.
Stanley Ho's syndicate STDM wins the gaming monopoly, launching modern casino tourism that transforms Macao's economy.
The 12-3 incident, leftist riots inspired by China's Cultural Revolution, weakens Portuguese authority and increases Beijing's influence.
After Portugal's Carnation Revolution, Lisbon offers to return Macao to China, but Beijing declines immediate handover.
Portugal grants Macao a new Organic Statute defining it as a special Chinese territory under Portuguese administration with broad autonomy.
Portugal and the People's Republic of China establish diplomatic relations, agreeing Macao is Chinese territory under Portuguese administration.
The Sino-Portuguese Joint Declaration is signed, providing for Macao's return to Chinese sovereignty in 1999 under 'one country, two systems'.
China's National People's Congress adopts the Macao Basic Law, the future constitutional framework guaranteeing autonomy for 50 years.
On 20 December, Portugal transfers sovereignty over Macao to China, which becomes a Special Administrative Region with Edmund Ho as Chief Executive.
The government ends Stanley Ho's monopoly by liberalizing the gaming industry, opening Macao to foreign casino operators.
The Sands Macao opens as the first foreign-owned casino, beginning a rapid expansion of the gaming sector.
The Historic Centre of Macao is inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its blend of Chinese and Portuguese cultures.
Macao overtakes the Las Vegas Strip in gaming revenue, becoming the world's largest gambling center.
The Venetian Macao opens on the reclaimed Cotai Strip, then one of the largest casino resorts in the world.
Macao's legislature passes a national security law under Article 23 of the Basic Law, ahead of similar moves elsewhere.
The Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macao Bridge, the world's longest sea crossing, opens, linking Macao to mainland China and Hong Kong.
Ho Iat Seng becomes Chief Executive as Macao marks the 20th anniversary of its return to China.
New 10-year gaming concessions are awarded, requiring operators to diversify Macao's economy beyond gambling amid post-pandemic recovery.
